quotes.text: My sins run out behind me, and I do not see them, and today I am coming to judge the errors of another.
quotes.author: Abba Moses
quotes.authorTitle: Desert Father
quotes.work: Sayings of the Desert Fathers (Apophthegmata Patrum)
quotes.sourceEdition: The Paradise of the Desert Fathers, EWTN Library (from Vitae Patrum tradition), public domain

quotes.text: Go and sit in your cell and your cell will teach you everything.
quotes.author: Abba Moses
quotes.authorTitle: Desert Father
quotes.work: Sayings of the Desert Fathers (Apophthegmata Patrum)
quotes.sourceEdition: The Paradise of the Desert Fathers, EWTN Library (from Vitae Patrum tradition), public domain

quotes.text: Behave as if you were a stranger, and wherever you are, do not expect your words to have an influence and you will be at peace.
quotes.author: Abba Poemen
quotes.authorTitle: Desert Father
quotes.work: Sayings of the Desert Fathers (Apophthegmata Patrum)
quotes.sourceEdition: The Paradise of the Desert Fathers, EWTN Library (from Vitae Patrum tradition), public domain

quotes.text: Humility and the fear of God are above all virtues.
quotes.author: Abba John the Dwarf
quotes.authorTitle: Desert Father
quotes.work: Sayings of the Desert Fathers (Apophthegmata Patrum)
quotes.sourceEdition: The Paradise of the Desert Fathers, EWTN Library (from Vitae Patrum tradition), public domain

quotes.text: I think that for those living in community obedience is a greater virtue than chastity, however perfect. Chastity carries within it the danger of pride, but obedience has within it the promise of humility.
quotes.author: Amma Syncletica
quotes.authorTitle: Desert Mother
quotes.work: Sayings of the Desert Fathers (Apophthegmata Patrum)
quotes.sourceEdition: The Paradise of the Desert Fathers, EWTN Library (from Vitae Patrum tradition), public domain

quotes.text: For the wilderness is the mother of quiet; it is a calm and a harbor, delivering us from all turmoils.
quotes.author: St. John Chrysostom
quotes.authorTitle: Archbishop of Constantinople, Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF First Series, Vol. 10, trans. George Prevost (1888), public domain

quotes.text: May the Father of the true light, Who has adorned day with celestial light, Who has made the fire to shine which illuminates us during the night, Who reserves for us in the peace of a future age a spiritual and everlasting light, enlighten your hearts in the knowledge of truth, keep you from stumbling, and grant that you may walk honestly as in the day.
quotes.author: St. Basil the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop of Caesarea, Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Second Series, Vol. 8, trans. Blomfield Jackson (1895), public domain

quotes.text: Worship a little in words, but more by your actions, and rather by keeping the Law than by admiring the Lawgiver; show your love for Him by fleeing from wickedness, pursuing after virtue, living in the Spirit, walking in the Spirit, drawing your knowledge from Him.
quotes.author: St. Gregory of Nazianzus
quotes.authorTitle: Archbishop of Constantinople, Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Second Series, Vol. 7, trans. Charles Gordon Browne and James Edward Swallow (1894), public domain

quotes.text: Nothing is worse than envy and malice, nothing more mischievous than vainglory; it is wont to mar ten thousand good things.
quotes.author: St. John Chrysostom
quotes.authorTitle: Archbishop of Constantinople, Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: Homilies on the Gospel of John
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF First Series, Vol. 14, trans. Philip Schaff (1889), public domain

quotes.text: When through love the mind is ravished by Divine knowledge and in going outside of creatures has a perception of Divine Transcendence, then it comes in consternation to a realization of its own lowliness.
quotes.author: St. Maximus the Confessor
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Confessor
quotes.sourceEdition: Trans. George C. Berthold, Paulist Press (1985); text also at saintandrewgoc.org

quotes.text: So then we ought to fear God only, and despise the demons, and be in no fear of them.
quotes.author: St. Athanasius
quotes.authorTitle: Archbishop of Alexandria, Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Second Series, Vol. 4, trans. H. Ellershaw (1892), public domain

quotes.text: And the Word was made flesh and tabernacled in us (and we saw His glory, the glory as of the Only-Begotten of the Father) full of grace and truth.
quotes.author: St. Cyril of Alexandria
quotes.authorTitle: Patriarch of Alexandria, Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Trans. P. E. Pusey (1881), reprinted at CCEL (pearse/morefathers), public domain

quotes.text: O Helmsman of that ark, be my pilot on the dry land! To it Thou gavest rest in the haven of a mountain; to me give Thou rest also in the haven of my walls!
quotes.author: St. Ephrem the Syrian
quotes.authorTitle: Deacon and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Second Series, Vol. 13, trans. J. T. Sarsfield Stopford (1898), public domain

quotes.text: Do not then go beyond yourself to seek for evil, and imagine that there is an original nature of wickedness.
quotes.author: St. Basil the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop of Caesarea, Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Second Series, Vol. 8, trans. Blomfield Jackson (1895), public domain

quotes.text: For piety is an insatiable thing; and it argues a childish mind to imagine from just the beginnings that you have obtained the whole.
quotes.author: St. John Chrysostom
quotes.authorTitle: Archbishop of Constantinople, Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF First Series, Vol. 12, trans. T. W. Chambers (1889), public domain

quotes.text: Take my advice, my friend, and be slow to do evil, but swift to your salvation; for readiness to evil and tardiness to good are equally bad.
quotes.author: St. Gregory of Nazianzus
quotes.authorTitle: Archbishop of Constantinople, Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Second Series, Vol. 7, trans. Charles Gordon Browne and James Edward Swallow (1894), public domain

quotes.text: For that death is destroyed, and that the Cross has become the victory over it, and that it has no more power but is verily dead, this is no small proof, or rather an evident warrant, that it is despised by all Christ's disciples, and that they all take the aggressive against it and no longer fear it; but by the sign of the Cross and by faith in Christ tread it down as dead.
quotes.author: St. Athanasius
quotes.authorTitle: Archbishop of Alexandria, Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: On the Incarnation of the Word
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Second Series, Vol. 4, trans. Archibald Robertson (1892), public domain

quotes.text: Change of place does not effect any drawing nearer unto God, but wherever you may be, God will come to you, if the chambers of your soul be found of such a sort that He can dwell in you and walk in you.
quotes.author: St. Gregory of Nyssa
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop of Nyssa, Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Second Series, Vol. 5, trans. W. Moore (1893), public domain

quotes.text: Our God is everywhere present, wholly everywhere; not confined to any place. He can be present unperceived, and be absent without moving; when He exposes us to adversities, it is either to prove our perfections or correct our imperfections; and in return for our patient endurance of the sufferings of time, He reserves for us an everlasting reward.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 2, trans. Marcus Dods (1887), public domain

quotes.text: You shall love God with all your heart, and with all your soul, and with all your mind: so that you are to concentrate all your thoughts, your whole life and your whole intelligence upon Him from whom you derive all that you bring.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 2, trans. J. F. Shaw (1887), public domain

quotes.text: Of all, then, that has been said since we entered upon the discussion about things, this is the sum: that we should clearly understand that the fulfillment and the end of the Law, and of all Holy Scripture, is the love of an object which is to be enjoyed, and the love of an object which can enjoy that other in fellowship with ourselves.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 2, trans. J. F. Shaw (1887), public domain

quotes.text: It is necessary, therefore, to purge our minds, in order to be able to see ineffably that which is ineffable; whereto not having yet attained, we are to be nourished by faith, and led by such ways as are more suited to our capacity, that we may be rendered apt and able to comprehend it.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 3, trans. Arthur West Haddan (1887), public domain

quotes.text: In that contemplation, therefore, God will be all in all; because nothing else but Himself will be required, but it will be sufficient to be enlightened by and to enjoy Him alone.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 3, trans. Arthur West Haddan (1887), public domain

quotes.text: Accordingly, two cities have been formed by two loves: the earthly by the love of self, even to the contempt of God; the heavenly by the love of God, even to the contempt of self. The former, in a word, glories in itself, the latter in the Lord.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 2, trans. Marcus Dods (1887), public domain

quotes.text: The will, therefore, is then truly free, when it is not the slave of vices and sins. Such was it given us by God; and this being lost by its own fault, can only be restored by Him who was able at first to give it. And therefore the truth says, If the Son shall make you free, you shall be free indeed.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 2, trans. Marcus Dods (1887), public domain

quotes.text: There we shall rest and see, see and love, love and praise. This is what shall be in the end without end.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 2, trans. Marcus Dods (1887), public domain

quotes.text: For peace is a good so great, that even in this earthly and mortal life there is no word we hear with such pleasure, nothing we desire with such zest, or find to be more thoroughly gratifying.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 2, trans. Marcus Dods (1887), public domain

quotes.text: With you is perfect rest, and life unchanging. He who enters into you enters into the joy of his Lord, and shall have no fear, and shall do excellently in the most Excellent.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 1, trans. J. G. Pilkington (1887), public domain

quotes.text: But You, my Love, in looking for whom I fail that I may be strong, art neither those bodies that we see, although in heaven, nor are You those which we see not there; for You have created them, nor do You reckon them among Your greatest works. But You are the life of souls, the life of lives, having life in Yourself; and You change not, O Life of my soul.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 1, trans. J. G. Pilkington (1887), public domain

quotes.text: O Thou Good Omnipotent, who so carest for every one of us as if You cared for him only, and so for all as if they were but one!
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 1, trans. J. G. Pilkington (1887), public domain

quotes.text: O mystery of piety! O sign of unity! O bond of charity!
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: Tractates on the Gospel of John
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 7, trans. John Gibb (1888), public domain

quotes.text: No greater gift could God have given to men than in making His Word, by which He created all things, their Head, and joining them to Him as His members: that the Son of God might become also the Son of man, one God with the Father, one Man with men; so that when we speak to God in prayer for mercy, we do not separate the Son from Him.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 8, trans. A. Cleveland Coxe (1888), public domain

quotes.text: Take this love, therefore, as the end that is set before you, to which you are to refer all that you say, and, whatever you narrate, narrate it in such a manner that he to whom you are discoursing on hearing may believe, on believing may hope, on hoping may love.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: On the Catechizing of the Uninstructed
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 3, trans. S. D. F. Salmond (1887), public domain

quotes.text: When we say: Your kingdom come, which shall certainly come whether we wish it or not, we do by these words stir up our own desires for that kingdom, that it may come to us, and that we may be found worthy to reign in it.
quotes.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series I, Vol. 1, trans. J. G. Cunningham (1887), public domain

quotes.text: He is wise, then, who knows how to keep silent. Lastly, the Wisdom of God said: The Lord has given to me the tongue of learning, that I should know when it is good to speak.
quotes.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: On the Duties of the Clergy
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 10, trans. H. De Romestin (1896), public domain

quotes.text: Mercy, also, is a good thing, for it makes men perfect, in that it imitates the perfect Father. Nothing graces the Christian soul so much as mercy; mercy as shown chiefly towards the poor, that you may treat them as sharers in common with you in the produce of nature, which brings forth the fruits of the earth for use to all.
quotes.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: On the Duties of the Clergy
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 10, trans. H. De Romestin (1896), public domain

quotes.text: When can the upright man be alone, since he is always with God? When is he left forsaken who is never separated from Christ?
quotes.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: On the Duties of the Clergy
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 10, trans. H. De Romestin (1896), public domain

quotes.text: Our life is hid with Christ in God. Let, then, no one here strive to shine, let none show pride, let none boast. Christ willed not to be known here, He would not that His Name should be preached in the Gospel while He lived on earth. He came to lie hidden from this world. Let us therefore likewise hide our life after the example of Christ, let us shun boastfulness, let us not desire to be made known. It is better to live here in humility, and there in glory.
quotes.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: On the Duties of the Clergy
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 10, trans. H. De Romestin (1896), public domain

quotes.text: Who though because of His nature He cannot be attained to, yet because of His goodness can be received by us, filling all things His power, but only partaken of by the just, simple in substance, rich in virtues, present to each, dividing of His own to every one, and Himself whole everywhere.
quotes.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 10, trans. H. De Romestin (1896), public domain

quotes.text: For as God shed forth of the Holy Spirit, so also the love of God is shed abroad in our hearts through the Holy Spirit; in order that we may understand that the Holy Spirit is not a work, Who is the dispenser and plenteous Fount of the divine love.
quotes.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 10, trans. H. De Romestin (1896), public domain

quotes.text: Blessed, plainly, is that life which is not valued at the estimation of outsiders, but is known, as judge of itself, by its own inner feelings. It needs no popular opinion as its reward in any way; nor has it any fear of punishments. Thus the less it strives for glory, the more it rises above it.
quotes.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: On the Duties of the Clergy
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 10, trans. H. De Romestin (1896), public domain

quotes.text: But the sacred Scriptures say that eternal life rests on a knowledge of divine things and on the fruit of good works. The Gospel bears witness to both these statements. For the Lord Jesus spoke thus of knowledge: This is eternal life, to know You, the only true God, and Jesus Christ Whom You have sent.
quotes.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: On the Duties of the Clergy
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 10, trans. H. De Romestin (1896), public domain

quotes.text: Love finds nothing hard; no task is difficult to the eager. Think of all that Jacob bore for Rachel, the wife who had been promised to him. And they seemed unto him but a few days for the love he had to her.
quotes.author: St. Jerome
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 6, trans. W. H. Fremantle (1893), public domain

quotes.text: As often as this life's idle show tries to charm you; as often as you see in the world some vain pomp, transport yourself in mind to Paradise, essay to be now what you will be hereafter.
quotes.author: St. Jerome
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 6, trans. W. H. Fremantle (1893), public domain

quotes.text: Read the divine scriptures constantly; never, indeed, let the sacred volume be out of your hand. Learn what you have to teach. Hold fast the faithful word as you have been taught that you may be able by sound doctrine to exhort and convince the gainsayers.
quotes.author: St. Jerome
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 6, trans. W. H. Fremantle (1893), public domain

quotes.text: Love the holy scriptures, and wisdom will love you. Love wisdom, and it will keep you safe. Honour wisdom, and it will embrace you round about.
quotes.author: St. Jerome
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 6, trans. W. H. Fremantle (1893), public domain

quotes.text: His members might learn to fly from the favours of the world, to be afraid of no terrors, to love adversity for the truth's sake, and to shrink in fear from prosperity; because this often defiles the heart through vain glory, while that purges it through sorrow; in this the mind exalts itself, but in that, even though it had once exalted itself, it brings itself low; in this man forgets himself, but in that, even perforce and against his will, he is recalled to memory of what he is.
quotes.author: St. Gregory the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. James Barmby (1895), public domain

quotes.text: The ruler should be a near neighbour to every one in sympathy, and exalted above all in contemplation, so that through the bowels of loving-kindness he may transfer the infirmities of others to himself, and by loftiness of speculation transcend even himself in his aspiration after the invisible.
quotes.author: St. Gregory the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. James Barmby (1895), public domain

quotes.text: But all this is duly executed by a ruler, if, inspired by the spirit of heavenly fear and love, he meditate daily on the precepts of Sacred Writ, that the words of Divine admonition may restore in him the power of solicitude and of provident circumspection with regard to the celestial life, which familiar intercourse with men continually destroys.
quotes.author: St. Gregory the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. James Barmby (1895), public domain

quotes.text: For that cock, too, whom the Lord in his manner of speech takes to represent a good preacher, when he is now preparing to crow, first shakes his wings, and by smiting himself makes himself more awake; since it is surely necessary that those who give utterance to words of holy preaching should first be well awake in earnestness of good living, lest they rouse others with their voice while themselves torpid in performance.
quotes.author: St. Gregory the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. James Barmby (1895), public domain

quotes.text: That man is indeed blind who is unacquainted with the light of supernal contemplation, who, whelmed in the darkness of the present life, while he beholds not at all by loving it the light to come, knows not whither he is advancing the steps of his conduct.
quotes.author: St. Gregory the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. James Barmby (1895), public domain

quotes.text: He who is the Imposer of the burden is Himself the Aider in its execution: and lest the weak recipient should fall beneath the greatness of the grace, He who conferred the dignity will also give the power.
quotes.author: St. Leo the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. Charles Lett Feltoe (1895), public domain

quotes.text: If, dearly beloved, we comprehend faithfully and wisely the beginning of our creation, we shall find that man was made in God's image, to the end that he might imitate his Creator, and that our race attains its highest natural dignity, by the form of the Divine goodness being reflected in us, as in a mirror.
quotes.author: St. Leo the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. Charles Lett Feltoe (1895), public domain

quotes.text: Thus it is that God, by loving us, restores us to His image, and, in order that He may find in us the form of His goodness, He gives us that whereby we ourselves too may do the work that He does, kindling that is the lamps of our minds, and inflaming us with the fire of His love, that we may love not only Himself, but also whatever He loves.
quotes.author: St. Leo the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. Charles Lett Feltoe (1895), public domain

quotes.text: When the body therefore fasts from food, let the mind fast from vices, and pass judgment upon all earthly cares and desires according to the law of its King.
quotes.author: St. Leo the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. Charles Lett Feltoe (1895), public domain

quotes.text: The Spirit of Truth Himself makes the house of His glory shine with the brightness of His light, and will have nothing dark nor lukewarm in His temple.
quotes.author: St. Leo the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. Charles Lett Feltoe (1895), public domain

quotes.text: Thus in the whole and perfect nature of true man was true God born, complete in what was His own, complete in what was ours.
quotes.author: St. Leo the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. Charles Lett Feltoe (1895), public domain

quotes.text: For He who is true God is also true man: and in this union there is no lie, since the humility of manhood and the loftiness of the Godhead both meet there.
quotes.author: St. Leo the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. Charles Lett Feltoe (1895), public domain

quotes.text: A man is justified by grace rather than by his own excellence: for grace is to every one the source of righteousness, the source of good and the fountain of merit.
quotes.author: St. Leo the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Pope and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 12, trans. Charles Lett Feltoe (1895), public domain

quotes.text: I know, O Lord God Almighty, that I owe You, as the chief duty of my life, the devotion of all my words and thoughts to Yourself.
quotes.author: St. Hilary of Poitiers
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 9, trans. E. W. Watson (1899), public domain

quotes.text: It is the Father to Whom all existence owes its origin. In Christ and through Christ He is the source of all. In contrast to all else He is self-existent. He does not draw His being from without, but possesses it from Himself and in Himself. He is infinite, for nothing contains Him and He contains all things; He is eternally unconditioned by space, for He is illimitable; eternally anterior to time, for time is His creation.
quotes.author: St. Hilary of Poitiers
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 9, trans. E. W. Watson (1899), public domain

quotes.text: This gift is with us unto the end of the world, the solace of our waiting, the assurance, by the favours which He bestows, of the hope that shall be ours, the light of our minds, the sun of our souls. This Holy Spirit we must seek and must earn, and then hold fast by faith and obedience to the commands of God.
quotes.author: St. Hilary of Poitiers
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 9, trans. E. W. Watson (1899), public domain

quotes.text: He does not cease to be God because He becomes man, nor fail to be man because He remains for ever God.
quotes.author: St. Hilary of Poitiers
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 9, trans. E. W. Watson (1899), public domain

quotes.text: God was born to take us into Himself, suffered to justify us, and died to avenge us; for our manhood abides for ever in Him, the weakness of our infirmity is united with His strength, and the spiritual powers of iniquity and wickedness are subdued in the triumph of our flesh, since God died through the flesh.
quotes.author: St. Hilary of Poitiers
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 9, trans. E. W. Watson (1899), public domain

quotes.text: He does not leave us in the vague and misleading paths of an indefinite teaching, or abandon us to the shifting fancies of imagination, but limits the unimpeded license of intellect and desire by the appointment of restraining barriers. He gives us no opportunity to be wise beyond what he preached, but defines in exact and precise language the faith fixed for all time.
quotes.author: St. Hilary of Poitiers
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 9, trans. E. W. Watson (1899), public domain

quotes.text: Moreover, in the Catholic Church itself, all possible care must be taken, that we hold that faith which has been believed everywhere, always, by all. For that is truly and in the strictest sense Catholic, which, as the name itself and the reason of the thing declare, comprehends all universally. This rule we shall observe if we follow universality, antiquity, consent.
quotes.author: St. Vincent of Lérins
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Confessor
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 11, trans. C. A. Heurtley (1894), public domain

quotes.text: To preach any doctrine therefore to Catholic Christians other than what they have received never was lawful, never is lawful, never will be lawful: and to anathematize those who preach anything other than what has once been received, always was a duty, always is a duty, always will be a duty.
quotes.author: St. Vincent of Lérins
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Confessor
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 11, trans. C. A. Heurtley (1894), public domain

quotes.text: For progress requires that the subject be enlarged in itself, alteration, that it be transformed into something else. The intelligence, then, the knowledge, the wisdom, as well of individuals as of all, as well of one man as of the whole Church, ought, in the course of ages and centuries, to increase and make much and vigorous progress; but yet only in its own kind; that is to say, in the same doctrine, in the same sense, and in the same meaning.
quotes.author: St. Vincent of Lérins
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Confessor
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 11, trans. C. A. Heurtley (1894), public domain

quotes.text: But whatsoever a teacher holds, other than all, or contrary to all, be he holy and learned, be he a bishop, be he a Confessor, be he a martyr, let that be regarded as a private fancy of his own, and be separated from the authority of common, public, general persuasion.
quotes.author: St. Vincent of Lérins
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Confessor
quotes.sourceEdition: NPNF Series II, Vol. 11, trans. C. A. Heurtley (1894), public domain

quotes.text: As soon as some infirmity overtakes him, a sick man should receive the Body and Blood of Christ, humbly and devoutly ask the presbyters for blessed oil, and anoint his body with it. Thus will be fulfilled in him what we read: 'Is anyone among you sick? Let him bring in the presbyters, and let them pray over him, anointing him with oil. And the prayer of faith will save the sick man, and the Lord will raise him up, and if he be in sins, they shall be forgiven him.' See to it, brethren, that a man hastens to the health and the remission of his sins.
quotes.author: St. Caesarius of Arles
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the Church Vol. 31, trans. Mary Magdeleine Mueller (CUA Press, 1956), public domain

quotes.text: The one who said, 'For the temple of God is holy, and you are that temple,' did not lie; likewise when he said: 'You must know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit, who is within you.' Now since we have merited to become the temple of God through His grace and without any preceding merits of our own, dearest brethren, let us put forth as much effort as we can, with His assistance, so that our Lord may not find in His temple, that is, in us, anything to offend the eyes of His majesty. Let the dwelling of our heart be freed of vices and filled with virtues; let it be closed to the devil and open to Christ.
quotes.author: St. Caesarius of Arles
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the Church Vol. 66, trans. Mary Magdeleine Mueller (CUA Press, 1973), public domain

quotes.text: May those, then, who are good, chaste, sober, humble, and gentle, persevere with God's help in their good works; may those who do evil correct themselves at once before their souls depart from this life. If they die without repentance they do not enter into life, but are cast into death.
quotes.author: St. Caesarius of Arles
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the Church Vol. 31, trans. Mary Magdeleine Mueller (CUA Press, 2004), public domain

quotes.text: There are three things, my brethren, by which faith stands firm, devotion remains constant, and virtue endures. They are prayer, fasting and mercy. Prayer knocks at the door, fasting obtains, mercy receives. Prayer, mercy and fasting: these three are one, and they give life to each other.
quotes.author: St. Peter Chrysologus
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Liturgy of the Hours, Office of Readings, public domain

quotes.text: Fasting is the soul of prayer, mercy is the lifeblood of fasting. Let no one try to separate them; they cannot be separated. If you have only one of them or not all together, you have nothing. So if you pray, fast; if you fast, show mercy; if you want your petition to be heard, hear the petition of others.
quotes.author: St. Peter Chrysologus
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Liturgy of the Hours, Office of Readings, public domain

quotes.text: Offer your soul to God, make him an oblation of your fasting, so that your soul may be a pure offering, a holy sacrifice, a living victim, remaining your own and at the same time made over to God. Whoever fails to give this to God will not be excused, for if you are to give him yourself you are never without the means of giving.
quotes.author: St. Peter Chrysologus
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Liturgy of the Hours, Office of Readings, public domain

quotes.text: Therefore it is necessary to arrive at a first mover, put in motion by no other; and this everyone understands to be God.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: This is part of the infinite goodness of God, that He should allow evil to exist, and out of it produce good.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.locus: Prima Pars, Q. 2, Art. 3, Reply to Obj. 1
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: God is in all things; not, indeed, as part of their essence, nor as an accident, but as an agent is present to that upon which it works.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: Therefore as long as a thing has being, God must be present to it, according to its mode of being.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: The love which is based on this communication, is charity: wherefore it is evident that charity is the friendship of man for God.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: Charity is the form of the virtues.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: Faith is a habit of the mind, whereby eternal life is begun in us, making the intellect assent to what is non-apparent.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: Final and perfect happiness can consist in nothing else than the vision of the Divine Essence.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: Therefore the last and perfect happiness, which we await in the life to come, consists entirely in contemplation.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: Prayer is the raising up of the mind to God.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: Nothing was so necessary for raising our hope as to show us how deeply God loved us. And what could afford us a stronger proof of this than that the Son of God should become a partner with us of human nature?
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: The presence of Christ's true body and blood in this sacrament cannot be detected by sense, nor understanding, but by faith alone, which rests upon Divine authority.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: Hence this is not a formal, but a substantial conversion; nor is it a kind of natural movement: but, with a name of its own, it can be called "transubstantiation."
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: Virtue is a good quality of the mind, by which we live righteously, of which no one can make bad use, which God works in us, without us.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: The contemplative life is to cling with our whole mind to the love of God and our neighbor, and to desire nothing beside our Creator.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation (1920), public domain

quotes.text: It belongs to the wise man to meditate especially on the truth belonging to the first principle and to teach it to others, so it belongs to him to refute the opposing falsehood.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Anton C. Pegis translation (1955), cited at isidore.co; public domain

quotes.text: Beneficially, therefore, did the divine Mercy provide that it should instruct us to hold by faith even those truths that the human reason is able to investigate.
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Anton C. Pegis translation (1955), cited at isidore.co; public domain

quotes.text: Hence, there is no doubt that there exists a being, than which nothing greater can be conceived, and it exists both in the understanding and in reality.
quotes.author: St. Anselm of Canterbury
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Sidney Norton Deane translation (1903), public domain

quotes.text: And so, Lord, do thou, who dost give understanding to faith, give me, so far as thou knowest it to be profitable, to understand that thou art as we believe; and that thou art that which we believe.
quotes.author: St. Anselm of Canterbury
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Sidney Norton Deane translation (1903), public domain

quotes.text: What art thou, except that which, as the highest of all beings, alone exists through itself, and creates all other things from nothing?
quotes.author: St. Anselm of Canterbury
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Sidney Norton Deane translation (1903), public domain

quotes.text: Thou hast found him to be a being which is the highest of all beings, a being than which nothing better can be conceived; that this being is life itself, light, wisdom, goodness, eternal blessedness and blessed eternity; and that it is every where and always.
quotes.author: St. Anselm of Canterbury
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Sidney Norton Deane translation (1903), public domain

quotes.text: Love the one good in which are all goods, and it sufficeth. Desire the simple good which is every good, and it is enough.
quotes.author: St. Anselm of Canterbury
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Sidney Norton Deane translation (1903), public domain

quotes.text: Until I enter into thy joy, O Lord, who art the Three and the One God, blessed for ever and ever. Amen.
quotes.author: St. Anselm of Canterbury
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Sidney Norton Deane translation (1903), public domain

quotes.text: Admit that God deserves to be loved very much, yea, boundlessly, because He loved us first, He infinite and we nothing, loved us, miserable sinners, with a love so great and so free.
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation (1950), CCEL, public domain

quotes.text: This is why I said at the beginning that the measure of our love to God is to love immeasurably.
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation (1950), CCEL, public domain

quotes.text: The motive for loving God is God Himself.
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation (1950), CCEL, public domain

quotes.text: True love does not demand a reward, but it deserves one.
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation (1950), CCEL, public domain

quotes.text: Rest is in Him alone.
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation (1950), CCEL, public domain

quotes.text: So it behoves us, if we would have Christ for a frequent guest, to fill our hearts with faithful meditations on the mercy He showed in dying for us, and on His mighty power in rising again from the dead.
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation (1950), CCEL, public domain

quotes.text: To the cry of prayer through Christ crucified, by Whose blood we are purged of the filth of vice, do I first invite the reader, lest perchance he should believe that it suffices to read without unction, speculate without devotion, investigate without wonder, examine without exultation, work without piety, know without love, understand without humility, be zealous without divine grace, see without wisdom divinely inspired.
quotes.author: St. Bonaventure
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: The Mind's Road to God (Itinerarium Mentis in Deum)
quotes.sourceEdition: George Boas translation (1953), Catholic Primer edition (2005), public domain

quotes.text: Since, relative to our life on earth, the world is itself a ladder for ascending to God, we find here certain traces of His hand, certain images, some corporeal, some spiritual, some temporal, some aeviternal; consequently some outside us, some inside.
quotes.author: St. Bonaventure
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: The Mind's Road to God (Itinerarium Mentis in Deum)
quotes.sourceEdition: George Boas translation (1953), Catholic Primer edition (2005), public domain

quotes.text: He, therefore, who is not illumined by such great splendor of created things is blind; he who is not awakened by such great clamor is deaf; he who does not praise God because of all these effects is dumb; he who does not note the First Principle from such great signs is foolish.
quotes.author: St. Bonaventure
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: The Mind's Road to God (Itinerarium Mentis in Deum)
quotes.sourceEdition: George Boas translation (1953), Catholic Primer edition (2005), public domain

quotes.text: Since beatitude is nothing else than the fruition of the highest good, and the highest good is above us, none can be made blessed unless he ascend above himself, not by the ascent of his body but by that of his heart.
quotes.author: St. Bonaventure
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: The Mind's Road to God (Itinerarium Mentis in Deum)
quotes.sourceEdition: George Boas translation (1953), Catholic Primer edition (2005), public domain

quotes.text: If you should ask how these things come about, question grace, not instruction; desire, not intellect; the cry of prayer, not pursuit of study; the spouse, not the teacher; God, not man; darkness, not clarity; not light, but the wholly flaming fire which will bear you aloft to God with fullest unction and burning affection.
quotes.author: St. Bonaventure
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: The Mind's Road to God (Itinerarium Mentis in Deum)
quotes.sourceEdition: George Boas translation (1953), Catholic Primer edition (2005), public domain

quotes.text: All mortal creatures in those anxious aims which find employment in so many varied pursuits, though they take many paths, yet strive to reach one goal—the goal of happiness.
quotes.author: St. Boethius
quotes.authorTitle: Philosopher and Martyr
quotes.sourceEdition: H.R. James translation (1897), Project Gutenberg, public domain

quotes.text: Since men become happy by the acquisition of happiness, while happiness is very Godship, it is manifest that they become happy by the acquisition of Godship.
quotes.author: St. Boethius
quotes.authorTitle: Philosopher and Martyr
quotes.sourceEdition: H.R. James translation (1897), Project Gutenberg, public domain

quotes.text: The common belief of all mankind agrees that God, the supreme of all things, is good. For since nothing can be imagined better than God, how can we doubt Him to be good than whom there is nothing better?
quotes.author: St. Boethius
quotes.authorTitle: Philosopher and Martyr
quotes.sourceEdition: H.R. James translation (1897), Project Gutenberg, public domain

quotes.text: Every fortune is good fortune; for it either rewards, disciplines, amends, or punishes, and so is either useful or just.
quotes.author: St. Boethius
quotes.authorTitle: Philosopher and Martyr
quotes.sourceEdition: H.R. James translation (1897), Project Gutenberg, public domain

quotes.text: Eternity is the possession of endless life whole and perfect at a single moment.
quotes.author: St. Boethius
quotes.authorTitle: Philosopher and Martyr
quotes.sourceEdition: H.R. James translation (1897), Project Gutenberg, public domain

quotes.text: Let us beg of God, who causes those who are of one mind to live in his Father's house, that He will vouchsafe, by his heavenly tokens, to declare to us, which tradition is to be followed; and by what means we are to find our way to his heavenly kingdom.
quotes.author: St. Bede the Venerable
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: Ecclesiastical History of the English People
quotes.sourceEdition: A.M. Sellar translation (1907), public domain

quotes.text: I always took delight in learning, or teaching, or writing.
quotes.author: St. Bede the Venerable
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: Ecclesiastical History of the English People
quotes.locus: Book IV, Chapter III (Bede's autobiographical note)
quotes.sourceEdition: CCEL / A.M. Sellar translation (1907), public domain

quotes.text: I know that angels visit the canonical hours and the congregations of the brethren. What if they do not find me among the brethren? May they not say, 'Where is Bede?'
quotes.author: St. Bede the Venerable
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: Ecclesiastical History of the English People
quotes.sourceEdition: CCEL / A.M. Sellar translation (1907), public domain

quotes.text: For your conduct as a king is based upon the knowledge which by orthodox preaching you have obtained of your God and Creator, whereby you believe and worship Him, and as far as man is able, pay Him the sincere devotion of your mind.
quotes.author: St. Bede the Venerable
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.work: Ecclesiastical History of the English People
quotes.sourceEdition: A.M. Sellar translation (1907), public domain

quotes.text: No virtue, my daughter, can have life in itself except through charity and humility, which is the foster-mother and nurse of charity.
quotes.author: St. Catherine of Siena
quotes.authorTitle: Virgin and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Algar Thorold translation (1907), public domain

quotes.text: The soul, who is lifted by a very great and yearning desire for the honour of God and the salvation of souls, begins by exercising herself, for a certain space of time, in the ordinary virtues, remaining in the cell of self-knowledge, in order to know better the goodness of God towards her.
quotes.author: St. Catherine of Siena
quotes.authorTitle: Virgin and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Algar Thorold translation (1907), public domain

quotes.text: Dost thou not know, dear daughter, that all the sufferings, which the soul endures, or can endure, in this life, are insufficient to punish one smallest fault, because the offence, being done to Me, who am the Infinite Good, calls for an infinite satisfaction?
quotes.author: St. Catherine of Siena
quotes.authorTitle: Virgin and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Algar Thorold translation (1907), public domain

quotes.text: O human, who are fragile dust of the earth and ashes of ashes! Cry out and speak of the origin of pure salvation until those people are instructed, who, though they see the inmost contents of the Scriptures, do not wish to tell them or preach them, because they are lukewarm and sluggish in serving God's justice.
quotes.author: St. Hildegard of Bingen
quotes.authorTitle: Virgin and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Columba Hart and Jane Bishop translation, text hosted at Columbia University; public domain excerpt

quotes.text: Burst forth into a fountain of abundance and overflow with mystical knowledge, until they who now think you contemptible because of Eve's transgression are stirred up by the flood of your irrigation.
quotes.author: St. Hildegard of Bingen
quotes.authorTitle: Virgin and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Columba Hart and Jane Bishop translation, text hosted at Columbia University; public domain excerpt

quotes.text: In studying nature we have not to inquire how God the Creator may, as He freely wills, use His creatures to work miracles and thereby show forth His power: we have rather to inquire what Nature with its immanent causes can naturally bring to pass.
quotes.author: St. Albert the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Quoted in the Catholic Encyclopedia article on Albertus Magnus, New Advent, public domain

quotes.text: Whoever believes that Aristotle was a god, must also believe that he never erred. But if one believe that Aristotle was a man, then doubtless he was liable to error just as we are.
quotes.author: St. Albert the Great
quotes.authorTitle: Bishop and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Quoted in the Catholic Encyclopedia article on Albertus Magnus, New Advent, public domain

quotes.text: Blessed art thou, my Lord, my God, and most beloved lover of my soul, who art one God in three persons.
quotes.author: St. Bridget of Sweden
quotes.authorTitle: Wife, Widow and Mystic
quotes.locus: Chapter I (Prayer of Praise and Thanksgiving)
quotes.sourceEdition: First English translation from the Antwerp edition (1611), public domain

quotes.text: O sweet Jesus, wound my heart, that tears of penitence and love may be my food night and day, and bring me entirely to thee; that my heart may ever be habitable for thee, and my conversation pleasing and acceptable to thee; and the end of my life so praiseworthy, that after the close of this life, I may deserve to praise thee with all thy Saints forever.
quotes.author: St. Bridget of Sweden
quotes.authorTitle: Wife, Widow and Mystic
quotes.locus: Chapter XXV (Prayers on the Passion of Our Lord, concluding prayer)
quotes.sourceEdition: First English translation from the Antwerp edition (1611), public domain

quotes.text: Of all things to be sought, the first is wisdom, in which the form of the perfect good consists.
quotes.author: Hugh of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Jerome Taylor translation (1961), ReadAncientTexts.com parallel edition, public domain

quotes.text: The highest solace in life is the study of wisdom, and he who finds it is fortunate, and he who possesses it is blessed.
quotes.author: Hugh of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Jerome Taylor translation (1961), ReadAncientTexts.com parallel edition, public domain

quotes.text: Two things indeed are what repair the divine similitude in man, that is, the speculation of truth and the exercise of virtue.
quotes.author: Hugh of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Jerome Taylor translation (1961), ReadAncientTexts.com parallel edition, public domain

quotes.text: The beginning of discipline is humility: first, that he hold no knowledge, no scripture as vile; second, that he not be ashamed to learn from anyone; third, that when he shall have attained knowledge, he not contemn the others.
quotes.author: Hugh of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Jerome Taylor translation (1961), ReadAncientTexts.com parallel edition, public domain

quotes.text: From all, learn gladly what you do not know, because humility can make common to you what nature has made proper to each. You will be wiser than all, if you are willing to learn from all.
quotes.author: Hugh of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Jerome Taylor translation (1961), ReadAncientTexts.com parallel edition, public domain

quotes.text: Meditation is frequent thinking with counsel, which prudently investigates the cause and origin, the mode and utility of each thing. Meditation takes its beginning from reading, yet is constrained by no rules or precepts of reading.
quotes.author: Hugh of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Jerome Taylor translation (1961), ReadAncientTexts.com parallel edition, public domain

quotes.text: Delicate is he still to whom the fatherland is sweet; strong already, he for whom every soil is a fatherland; but perfect, he for whom the whole world is exile.
quotes.author: Hugh of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Jerome Taylor translation (1961), ReadAncientTexts.com parallel edition, public domain

quotes.text: Learn all things; afterward you will see that nothing is superfluous. Constricted knowledge is not pleasant.
quotes.author: Hugh of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Jerome Taylor translation (1961), ReadAncientTexts.com parallel edition, public domain

quotes.text: Therefore, about to build, first set the foundation of history, then, through typological signification, raise the fabric of the mind into the citadel of faith.
quotes.author: Hugh of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Jerome Taylor translation (1961), ReadAncientTexts.com parallel edition, public domain

quotes.text: By the tabernacle of the covenant we understand the state of perfection. Where perfection of the soul is, there also is the habitation of God.
quotes.author: Richard of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Grover A. Zinn translation (1979), cited at catholicgnosis.wordpress.com; public domain

quotes.text: A person enters into the first tabernacle when he returns to himself. A person enters into the second tabernacle when he goes beyond himself. When going beyond himself surely a person is elevated to God.
quotes.author: Richard of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Grover A. Zinn translation (1979), cited at catholicgnosis.wordpress.com; public domain

quotes.text: By the Ark of the Covenant we understand the grace of contemplation. An ark is a secret place for gold and silver; the grace of contemplation lays hold of the treasury of celestial wisdom.
quotes.author: Richard of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.locus: Book II (on the Ark as contemplation)
quotes.sourceEdition: Grover A. Zinn translation (1979), cited at catholicgnosis.wordpress.com; public domain

quotes.text: A candelabrum is a holder for lights; discretion is the lamp of the inner person.
quotes.author: Richard of St. Victor
quotes.authorTitle: Canon Regular and Theologian
quotes.sourceEdition: Grover A. Zinn translation (1979), cited at catholicgnosis.wordpress.com; public domain

quotes.text: Whosoever praises God for His essential goodness, and not merely because of the benefits He has bestowed, does really love God for God’s sake, and not selfishly.
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation, CCEL

quotes.text: In such wise man, animal and carnal by nature, and loving only himself, begins to love God by reason of that very self-love; since he learns that in God he can accomplish all things that are good, and that without God he can do nothing.
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation, CCEL

quotes.text: How blessed is he who reaches the fourth degree of love, wherein one loves himself only in God!
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation, CCEL

quotes.text: O chaste and holy love! O sweet and gracious affection! O pure and cleansed purpose, thoroughly washed and purged from any admixture of selfishness, and sweetened by contact with the divine will!
quotes.author: St. Bernard of Clairvaux
quotes.authorTitle: Abbot and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: A Religious of C.S.M.V. translation, CCEL

quotes.text: By this light the blessed are made “deiform”—i.e. like to God, according to the saying: “When He shall appear we shall be like to Him, and we shall see Him as He is” (1 John 3:2).
quotes.author: St. Thomas Aquinas
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Doctor of the Church
quotes.sourceEdition: Fathers of the English Dominican Province translation, New Advent

quotes.text: To be holy is, in our Church's words, to have 'the true circumcision of the Spirit;' that is, to be separate from sin, to hate the works of the world, the flesh, and the devil; to take pleasure in keeping God's commandments; to do things as He would have us do them; to live habitually as in the sight of the world to come, as if we had broken the ties of this life, and were dead already.
quotes.author: St. John Henry Newman
quotes.authorTitle: Cardinal and Confessor
quotes.locus: Vol. I, Sermon 1: Holiness Necessary for Future Blessedness
quotes.sourceEdition: Newman Reader edition (newmanreader.org), public domain

quotes.text: To understand that we have souls, is to feel our separation from things visible, our independence of them, our distinct existence in ourselves, our individuality, our power of acting for ourselves this way or that way, our accountableness for what we do.
quotes.author: St. John Henry Newman
quotes.authorTitle: Cardinal and Confessor
quotes.locus: Vol. I, Sermon 2: The Immortality of the Soul
quotes.sourceEdition: Newman Reader edition (newmanreader.org), public domain

quotes.text: A habit of mind is formed which lasts through life, of which the attributes are, freedom, equitableness, calmness, moderation, and wisdom; or what in a former Discourse I have ventured to call a philosophical habit.
quotes.author: St. John Henry Newman
quotes.authorTitle: Cardinal and Confessor
quotes.locus: Discourse V: Knowledge Its Own End
quotes.sourceEdition: Newman Reader edition (newmanreader.org), public domain

quotes.text: That only is true enlargement of mind which is the power of viewing many things at once as one whole, of referring them severally to their true place in the universal system, of understanding their respective values, and determining their mutual dependence.
quotes.author: St. John Henry Newman
quotes.authorTitle: Cardinal and Confessor
quotes.locus: Discourse VI: Knowledge Viewed in Relation to Learning
quotes.sourceEdition: Newman Reader edition (newmanreader.org), public domain

quotes.text: God has created all things for good; all things for their greatest good; everything for its own good.
quotes.author: St. John Henry Newman
quotes.authorTitle: Cardinal and Confessor
quotes.locus: Part III, Section I: Hope in God — Creator
quotes.sourceEdition: Newman Reader edition (newmanreader.org), public domain

quotes.text: For ourselves, let us but obey God's voice in our hearts, and I will venture to say we shall have no doubts practically formidable about the truth of Scripture.
quotes.author: St. John Henry Newman
quotes.authorTitle: Cardinal and Confessor
quotes.locus: Vol. I, Sermon 15: Religious Faith Rational
quotes.sourceEdition: Newman Reader edition (newmanreader.org), public domain

quotes.text: The madman is not the man who has lost his reason. The madman is the man who has lost everything except his reason.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg eBook #16769, public domain

quotes.text: Mysticism keeps men sane; when you destroy mystery you create morbidity.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg eBook #16769, public domain

quotes.text: The circle returns upon itself and is bound. The cross opens its arms to the four winds; it is a signpost for free travellers.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg eBook #16769, public domain

quotes.text: The world was a shock, but it was not merely shocking; existence was a surprise, but it was a pleasant surprise.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.locus: Chapter IV: The Ethics of Elfland
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg eBook #16769, public domain

quotes.text: The three mystical virtues which Christianity has not adopted, but invented, are faith, hope, and charity.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.locus: Chapter 12: Paganism and Mr. Lowes Dickinson
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg eBook #470, public domain

quotes.text: There is no such thing on earth as an uninteresting subject; the only thing that can exist is an uninterested person.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg eBook #470, public domain

quotes.text: We are Christians and Catholics not because we worship a key, but because we have passed a door; and felt the wind that is the trumpet of liberty blow over the land of the living.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.locus: Part II, Chapter IV: The Witness of the Heretics
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg Australia eBook (gutenberg.net.au), public domain

quotes.text: It was the first day of a new creation, with a new heaven and a new earth; and in a semblance of the gardener God walked again in the garden, in the cool not of the evening but the dawn.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.locus: Part II, Chapter III: The Strangest Story in the World
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg Australia eBook (gutenberg.net.au), public domain

quotes.text: Nobody will begin to understand the Thomist philosophy, or indeed the Catholic philosophy, who does not realise that the primary and fundamental part of it is entirely the praise of Life, the praise of Being, the praise of God as the Creator of the World.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.locus: Chapter IV: A Meditation on the Manichees
quotes.sourceEdition: The Catholic Primer edition (archive.org), public domain

quotes.text: St. Thomas did not reconcile Christ to Aristotle; he reconciled Aristotle to Christ.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.sourceEdition: The Catholic Primer edition (archive.org), public domain

quotes.text: He knew that the praise of God stands on its strongest ground when it stands on nothing.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.locus: Chapter VIII: The Mirror of Christ
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg eBook #63084, public domain

quotes.text: The whole point of St. Francis of Assisi is that he certainly was ascetical and he certainly was not gloomy.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.locus: Chapter VIII: The Mirror of Christ
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg eBook #63084, public domain

quotes.text: What is wrong is that we do not ask what is right.
quotes.author: G. K. Chesterton
quotes.authorTitle: Author and apologist
quotes.locus: Part I, Chapter I: The Medical Mistake
quotes.sourceEdition: Project Gutenberg eBook #1717, public domain

quotes.text: Man cannot live without love. He remains a being that is incomprehensible for himself, his life is senseless, if love is not revealed to him, if he does not encounter love, if he does not experience it and make it his own, if he does not participate intimately in it.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1979)

quotes.text: THE REDEEMER OF MAN, Jesus Christ, is the centre of the universe and of history.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1979)

quotes.text: The splendour of truth shines forth in all the works of the Creator and, in a special way, in man, created in the image and likeness of God.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1993)

quotes.text: Following Christ is thus the essential and primordial foundation of Christian morality.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1993)

quotes.text: Faith and reason are like two wings on which the human spirit rises to the contemplation of truth; and God has placed in the human heart a desire to know the truth—in a word, to know himself—so that, by knowing and loving God, men and women may also come to the fullness of truth about themselves.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1998)

quotes.text: Man is called to a fullness of life which far exceeds the dimensions of his earthly existence, because it consists in sharing the very life of God.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1995)

quotes.text: It is precisely Mary's faith which marks the beginning of the new and eternal Covenant of God with man in Jesus Christ; this heroic faith of hers 'precedes' the apostolic witness of the Church, and ever remains in the Church's heart hidden like a special heritage of God's revelation.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1987)

quotes.text: Family, become what you are.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1981)

quotes.text: Humanity is loved by God! This very simple yet profound proclamation is owed to humanity by the Church. Each Christian's words and life must make this proclamation resound: God loves you, Christ came for you, Christ is for you 'the Way, the Truth and the Life!'
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1988)

quotes.text: Open wide the doors for Christ.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.work: Homily at the Beginning of the Pontificate
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (1978)

quotes.text: We are an Easter People and Alleluia is our song!
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official text (1986)

quotes.text: Do not be afraid to go out on the streets and into public places, like the first Apostles who preached Christ and the Good News of salvation in the squares of cities, towns and villages.
quotes.author: St. John Paul II
quotes.authorTitle: Pope
quotes.work: Homily at World Youth Day, Denver
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English text (1993)

quotes.text: Love is the light—and in the end, the only light—that can always illuminate a world grown dim and give us the courage needed to keep living and working.
quotes.author: Pope Benedict XVI
quotes.authorTitle: Pope Emeritus
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (2005)

quotes.text: The one who has hope lives differently; the one who hopes has been granted the gift of a new life.
quotes.author: Pope Benedict XVI
quotes.authorTitle: Pope Emeritus
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (2007)

quotes.text: God is the foundation of hope: not any god, but the God who has a human face and who has loved us to the end, each one of us and humanity in its entirety.
quotes.author: Pope Benedict XVI
quotes.authorTitle: Pope Emeritus
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (2007)

quotes.text: Do not be afraid of Christ! He takes nothing away, and he gives you everything.
quotes.author: Pope Benedict XVI
quotes.authorTitle: Pope Emeritus
quotes.work: Homily for the Inauguration of the Pontificate
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English text (2005)

quotes.text: It is not power, but love that redeems us! This is God's sign: he himself is love.
quotes.author: Pope Benedict XVI
quotes.authorTitle: Pope Emeritus
quotes.work: Homily for the Inauguration of the Pontificate
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English text (2005)

quotes.text: Only in this friendship are the doors of life opened wide. Only in this friendship is the great potential of human existence truly revealed. Only in this friendship do we experience beauty and liberation.
quotes.author: Pope Benedict XVI
quotes.authorTitle: Pope Emeritus
quotes.work: Homily for the Inauguration of the Pontificate
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English text (2005)

quotes.text: The word of God makes us change our concept of realism: the realist is the one who recognizes in the word of God the foundation of all things.
quotes.author: Pope Benedict XVI
quotes.authorTitle: Pope Emeritus
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (2010)

quotes.text: A humanism which excludes God is an inhuman humanism.
quotes.author: Pope Benedict XVI
quotes.authorTitle: Pope Emeritus
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English translation (2009)

quotes.text: It is only by conforming our own will to the divine one that human beings attain their true height, that they become 'divine'; only by coming out of ourselves, only in the 'yes' to God, is Adam's desire — and the desire of us all — to be completely free.
quotes.author: Pope Benedict XVI
quotes.authorTitle: Pope Emeritus
quotes.sourceEdition: Vatican.va official English text (2012)

quotes.text: Love begins at home, and it is not how much we do, but how much love we put in the action that we do.
quotes.author: St. Teresa of Calcutta
quotes.authorTitle: Virgin and Foundress
quotes.sourceEdition: NobelPrize.org transcript (1979)

quotes.text: We are not real social workers. We may be doing social work in the eyes of the people, but we are really contemplatives in the heart of the world.
quotes.author: St. Teresa of Calcutta
quotes.authorTitle: Virgin and Foundress
quotes.sourceEdition: NobelPrize.org transcript (1979)

quotes.text: He makes himself the hungry one – the naked one – the homeless one – the sick one – the one in prison – the lonely one – the unwanted one – and he says: You did it to me.
quotes.author: St. Teresa of Calcutta
quotes.authorTitle: Virgin and Foundress
quotes.sourceEdition: NobelPrize.org transcript (1979)

quotes.text: Let us always meet each other with a smile, for the smile is the beginning of love.
quotes.author: St. Teresa of Calcutta
quotes.authorTitle: Virgin and Foundress
quotes.sourceEdition: NobelPrize.org transcript (1979)

quotes.text: Prayer is nothing else than union with God.
quotes.author: St. John Vianney
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Confessor (Curé d'Ars)
quotes.sourceEdition: EWTN Library, Instructions on the Catechism (translation, public domain text)

quotes.text: To be saved, we must know, love and serve God. Oh, what a beautiful life! How good, how great a thing it is to know, to love and serve God! We have nothing else to do in this world. All that we do besides is lost time.
quotes.author: St. John Vianney
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Confessor (Curé d'Ars)
quotes.sourceEdition: EWTN Library, Instructions on the Catechism (translation, public domain text)

quotes.text: The Holy Spirit is light and strength. He teaches us to distinguish between truth and falsehood, and between good and evil.
quotes.author: St. John Vianney
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Confessor (Curé d'Ars)
quotes.sourceEdition: EWTN Library, Instructions on the Catechism (translation, public domain text)

quotes.text: O God! the happiness Thou promisest us is such that the eyes of man cannot see it, his ears cannot hear it, nor his heart conceive it.
quotes.author: St. John Vianney
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Confessor (Curé d'Ars)
quotes.sourceEdition: EWTN Library, Instructions on the Catechism (translation, public domain text)

quotes.text: Don't let your life be barren. Be useful. Make yourself felt. Shine forth with the torch of your faith and your love. With your apostolic life, wipe out the trail of filth and slime left by the corrupt sowers of hatred. And set aflame all the ways of the earth with the fire of Christ that you bear in your heart.
quotes.author: St. Josemaría Escrivá
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Founder of Opus Dei
quotes.sourceEdition: escrivaworks.org, © Studium Foundation (authorised digital edition)

quotes.text: How I wish your bearing and conversation were such that, on seeing or hearing you, people would say: This man reads the life of Jesus Christ.
quotes.author: St. Josemaría Escrivá
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Founder of Opus Dei
quotes.sourceEdition: escrivaworks.org, © Studium Foundation (authorised digital edition)

quotes.text: Action is worth nothing without prayer: prayer grows in value with sacrifice.
quotes.author: St. Josemaría Escrivá
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Founder of Opus Dei
quotes.sourceEdition: escrivaworks.org, © Studium Foundation (authorised digital edition)

quotes.text: To pray is to talk with God. But about what? About Him, about yourself: joys, sorrows, successes and failures, noble ambitions, daily worries, weaknesses! And acts of thanksgiving and petitions: and Love and reparation. In a word: to get to know him and to get to know yourself: 'to get acquainted!'
quotes.author: St. Josemaría Escrivá
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Founder of Opus Dei
quotes.sourceEdition: escrivaworks.org, © Studium Foundation (authorised digital edition)

quotes.text: If you don't deny yourself you will never be a soul of prayer.
quotes.author: St. Josemaría Escrivá
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Founder of Opus Dei
quotes.sourceEdition: escrivaworks.org, © Studium Foundation (authorised digital edition)

quotes.text: Don't wish to be like the gilded weather-cock on top of a great building: however much it shines, and however high it stands, it adds nothing to the solidity of the building. Rather be like an old stone block hidden in the foundations, underground, where no one can see you: because of you the house will not fall.
quotes.author: St. Josemaría Escrivá
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Founder of Opus Dei
quotes.sourceEdition: escrivaworks.org, © Studium Foundation (authorised digital edition)

quotes.text: The cheerfulness you should have is not the kind we might call physiological good spirits — the happiness of a healthy animal. You must seek something more: the supernatural happiness that comes from the abandonment of everything and the abandonment of yourself into the loving arms of our Father-God.
quotes.author: St. Josemaría Escrivá
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Founder of Opus Dei
quotes.sourceEdition: escrivaworks.org, © Studium Foundation (authorised digital edition)

quotes.text: To begin is easy; to persevere is sanctity. Let your perseverance not be a blind consequence of the first impulse, the work of inertia: let it be a reflective perseverance.
quotes.author: St. Josemaría Escrivá
quotes.authorTitle: Priest and Founder of Opus Dei
quotes.sourceEdition: escrivaworks.org, © Studium Foundation (authorised digital edition)

quotes.text: We have all known the long loneliness and we have learned that the only solution is love and that love comes with community.
quotes.author: Servant of God Dorothy Day
quotes.authorTitle: Servant of God and Catholic Worker founder
quotes.sourceEdition: Harper & Row, 1952; cited in Wikiquote

quotes.text: The greatest challenge of the day is: how to bring about a revolution of the heart, a revolution which has to start with each one of us?
quotes.author: Servant of God Dorothy Day
quotes.authorTitle: Servant of God and Catholic Worker founder
quotes.sourceEdition: Harper & Row, 1963; cited in Wikiquote

quotes.text: God meant things to be much easier than we have made them. A man has a natural right to food, clothing, and shelter. A certain amount of goods is necessary to lead a good life. A family needs work as well as bread. Property is proper to man. We must keep repeating these things. Eternal life begins now.
quotes.author: Servant of God Dorothy Day
